title: Executive Order 10560—Administration of the Agricultural Trade Development and Assistance Act of 1954

flags.justification: Functions are extensively distributed across Agriculture, the Foreign Operations Administration, State, Treasury, the Bureau of the Budget, Defense Mobilization, and Defense, each tied to specific statutory subsections.
Sec. 5 explicitly reserves congressional-reporting functions to the President rather than delegating them away.
Six distinct functional delegations all coherently implement one comprehensive statute.
A delegation-of-functions order implementing an existing statute, not creating new substantive standards.
No compliance regime is created by this order.
A standing delegation for an ongoing statutory program.
Sec. 3(b) explicitly subordinates all delegated functions to the Secretary of State's foreign-policy responsibilities, consistent with EO 10464's design.
No third-party compliance regime is created by this delegation order.

Implements Public Law 480, the 'Food for Peace' program, one of the most historically significant agricultural and foreign-aid programs in U.S. history, shaping agricultural surplus policy and international food-security assistance for decades.
An extensive but carefully coordinated, statute-anchored division of roles across many agencies.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 1/12 = 8.3%. Implements PL-480, a program of enormous and lasting historical significance, yet scores low due to extensive, careful, cross-agency-coordinated distribution of authority combined with an explicit reservation of congressional-reporting accountability and a foreign-policy cross-check — a third example this batch, alongside EO 10426 and EO 10521, of genuinely significant orders built with well-distributed, protective architecture.
